Introduction
Over the past year, I’ve had the opportunity to work on several data engineering and AI-focused projects. One of the most interesting challenges involved document processing.
While the business domains were completely different — healthcare-related documents in one case and land deed processing in another — the underlying problem was surprisingly similar.
Organizations were receiving documents in multiple formats, layouts, and structures. Humans could understand these documents easily, but downstream systems could not consume them efficiently because the information existed in an unstructured format.
The challenge was not simply extracting text from documents.
The challenge was transforming inconsistent documents into reliable, structured datasets that could power applications, analytics, and business workflows.
In this article, I’ll walk through the architecture, key design decisions, and lessons learned while building a document intelligence proof of concept using Snowflake AI capabilities.
The Business Challenge
Most organizations accumulate thousands of documents every day:

• Invoices

• Receipts

• Land deeds

• Insurance documents

• Contracts

• Forms
Although these documents contain valuable information, extracting that information consistently is difficult because:

• Layouts vary between vendors

• Image quality differs

• Fields appear in different locations

• Some documents follow no fixed structure
Traditional rule-based extraction often becomes difficult to maintain as document variations increase.
This is where AI-assisted extraction becomes valuable.
Architecture
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Documents arrive in Amazon S3 and are exposed to Snowflake through an External Stage. An event-driven ingestion layer can detect newly arriving files and initiate downstream processing workflows. Snowflake AI capabilities are then used to extract structured information from the documents. The extracted output flows through RAW, CLEAN, and CURATED layers before being made available to applications, APIs, and analytical workloads.
Why Snowflake AI Features?
One aspect I enjoyed exploring was Snowflake’s growing AI ecosystem.
During the proof of concept, we evaluated multiple approaches:
AI_PARSE_DOCUMENT
Useful for extracting content and structure from documents.
Strengths:

• Native Snowflake experience

• Quick implementation

• Structured extraction capabilities
Limitations:

• Performance varied across highly inconsistent document formats.

AI_COMPLETE
During experimentation, AI_COMPLETE produced the most flexible results for highly inconsistent document formats.
By combining prompt engineering with structured output instructions, we were able to guide extraction and map responses into predefined schemas.
This significantly reduced the amount of post-processing required compared to some of the earlier approaches we evaluated.
It also aligned well with a schema-driven extraction approach, allowing the model output to be mapped into structured datasets for downstream processing.

Building the Data Pipeline
The extraction process was only one piece of the solution.
The complete pipeline involved several layers:
To make the pipeline scalable and maintainable, we adopted a Medallion-style architecture consisting of RAW, CLEAN, and CURATED layers. This approach helped separate AI extraction, data validation, and business-ready consumption concerns while improving traceability, reprocessing capabilities, and downstream reliability.
Rather than allowing applications to directly consume AI-generated outputs, each layer progressively improved the quality and usability of the data before exposing it to reporting, analytics, and operational systems.
RAW Layer
Store extraction results exactly as returned by the AI model.
Benefits:

• Auditability

• Traceability

• Reprocessing support
